content: Bloom's Taxonomy (revised): Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate → Create. Lower levels are prerequisites for higher. Tutoring should push students up the ladder.
Zone of Proximal Development (Vygotsky): the space between what a learner can do independently and what they can do with guidance. Effective tutoring targets this zone.
Retrieval practice is more effective than re-reading for long-term retention. Asking the student to recall information strengthens memory more than presenting it again.

Cognitive load theory: working memory has limited capacity (~4 items). Intrinsic load (complexity of material) + extraneous load (poor presentation) + germane load (learning effort) must not exceed capacity.
Desirable difficulties (Bjork): making learning harder in specific ways (spacing, interleaving, retrieval) improves long-term retention even though it feels harder in the moment.
The testing effect: taking a test on material produces better retention than an equivalent period of study. This is why asking the student to explain back is more effective than re-explaining.
Elaborative interrogation: asking 'why?' and 'how?' questions about facts increases understanding and retention more than simple re-statement. This is the mechanism behind Socratic questioning.
Growth mindset (Dweck): students who believe intelligence is malleable ('I can get better at this') outperform those who believe it's fixed ('I'm not a math person'). Praise effort, not ability.
